other_observers: Unspecified others; No independent observers were allowed at the request of the landowner.
duration: Total time of observations over approximately 3 weeks: about 25 minutes.
description: Description of the males: The birds were dark, long and slender sparrows. The head was gray with extensive black on the anterior of the face. The black lores connected with the black chin to create a patch of black on the otherwise gray head. The bill was pink with a slight reddish cast. The bill was petite for a sparrow. The back was rufous with distinct dark streaking. The rufous back was often visible on the birds in flight. The wings were brown. The tail was long and gray. The underparts were mostly gray. Some white was visible on the belly.  Description of the females: The females were very similar. The main differences were that the black lores were inconspicuous and the black chin was less extensive.Behaviors: The males were singing from exposed song perches on taller shrubs. (A favorite perch was on dead branches that extended above the green vegetation of a serviceberry bush.) At one time on 6/28/10, a male that initially perched to sing was followed (not chased) by a second bird of unknown gender. The tops of junipers were song perches on 7/5/10. The last 2 weeks that the birds were seen (7/10/10 and 7/20/10), the males were still singing, but not as much and from lower perches (lower shrubs, i.e., oak and sagebrush). One female was seen in the afternoon of 6/28/10 by a second observer. The appearance of that female occurred after pishing. A likely conclusion could be that a nest was approached. On 7/20/10, I saw 2 females (about 120 meters apart from each other). These females were staying low and difficult to observe.Call: In the morning, the bird sang one introductory note, followed by a descending note and then a trill. The trill resembled the sound of an accelerating, bouncing ball. Between 2:00 PM and 2:25 PM, the song started with a descending whistle. The whistle was followed by the "bouncing ball" sequence. A third song was sung in that same time period. This song had 3 introductory notes which were short and similar in pitch. The songs always ended with the accelerating thrill.Plumage: adult
